title: A superconducting surface-code processor with lattice-surgery logical operations
authors: Yanzhe Wang, Fanhao Shen, Haipeng Xie, Aosai Zhang, Yu Gao, Chuanyu Zhang, Xuhao Zhu, Feitong Jin, Yiren Zou, Ning Wang, Zhengyi Cui, Zehang Bao, Zitian Zhu, Jiarun Zhong, Gongyu Liu, Jia-Nan Yang, Yihang Han, Yiyang He, Jiayuan Shen, Han Wang, Jiahua Huang, Xinrong Zhang, Sailang Zhou, Hang Dong, Jinfeng Deng, Yaozu Wu, Zixuan Song, Hekang Li, Zhen Wang, Chao Song, Qiujiang Guo, Pengfei Zhang, H. Wang, Ying Li
abstract: Fault-tolerant logical operations are fundamental for scalable quantum computation. Here, we report the experimental realization of lattice-surgery operations between a pair of distance-three surface-code logical qubits on a planar superconducting processor. During repeated syndrome extraction cycles, the logical qubits exhibit per-cycle error rates of $0.0365(2)$ and $0.0282(1)$, respectively, after leakage events are rejected. By leveraging joint initialization and lattice splitting, we deterministically prepare a logical Bell state, confirming genuine bipartite entanglement via the error-corrected logical state fidelity. We further execute a two-qubit Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm at the logical level to demonstrate algorithmic utility in a fault-tolerant framework. Finally, to achieve universal control, we implement magic-state injection and gate teleportation to realize continuous non-Clifford rotations about the logical $X$ axis. For the logical $R_{X}(π/4)$ gate, we achieve a logical gate fidelity of $0.943_{-9}^{+10}$ conditioned on the absence of detected errors. These results establish lattice surgery as a practical and versatile paradigm for logical computation in near-term surface-code architectures, representing a critical milestone toward scalable fault-tolerant quantum advantage in superconducting circuits.
summary: This paper reports an experimental realization of lattice-surgery operations on a superconducting surface code processor. By performing syndrome extraction cycles and utilizing advanced techniques like magic-state injection, the researchers demonstrated fault-tolerant logical computation. The results establish lattice surgery as a practical method for achieving high-fidelity quantum gates necessary for scalable quantum computing.
main_problem: The primary goal is demonstrating fault-tolerant logical operations, specifically lattice-surgery, between superconducting surface-code logical qubits to advance scalable quantum computation.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated lattice-surgery, achieving a high logical gate fidelity of 0.943 for an RX(pi/4) rotation, validating this paradigm for fault-tolerant circuits in superconducting platforms.
method: The work involves repeated syndrome extraction cycles (SECs), deterministic state preparation via lattice splitting, executing algorithms like Deutsch-Jozsa, and implementing universal gates using magic-state injection and gate teleportation.
model_or_system: The experiment is performed on a planar superconducting processor utilizing transmon qubits configured into distance-three surface-code patches. The system supports nearest-neighbor couplings and uses techniques like CPMG decoupling to protect data qubits.
key_observables: Logical error rates (e.g., 0.0365 per cycle), logical Bell state fidelity (up to 0.925 post-selection), and the gate fidelity for non-Clifford rotations ($	ext{RX}(\pi/4)$).
important_parameters: Distance of the code (d=3), physical error probabilities (e.g., CZ gate fidelity $\approx 0.996$), and coherence times ($T_1 \approx 45 \mu	ext{s}$).
main_assumptions: The high logical fidelities are conditioned on the absence of detected errors, and the current implementation relies heavily on post-selection rather than fully active error correction.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental layout, conceptual lattice-merge/split operations, decay curves showing logical memory performance over SECs, and circuit diagrams detailing algorithm execution and gate teleportation protocols.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from characterizing physical hardware fidelity (gate errors, coherence times) to demonstrating basic error correction via syndrome extraction cycles, then implementing complex logical tasks like Bell state generation and Deutsch-Jozsa, culminating in the realization of universal gates via lattice surgery.
why_interesting: For a theoretical physicist, this work provides crucial experimental benchmarks for fault tolerance thresholds and validates specific physical protocols (like lattice surgery) that can be modeled theoretically to predict scaling behavior towards quantum advantage.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental layout and device performance. a, Qubit connectivity used for lattice surgery. The lattice comprises data qubits (gold circles), X-type syndrome qubits (red circles), and Z-type syndrome qubits (blue circles). Each syndrome qubit is coupled to two or four neighboring data qubits, facilitating the stabilizer measurements. b, Conceptual illustration of lattice-merge and lattice-split operations. Two distance-three surface-code patches, denoted as L1 and L2, are embedded in a two-dimensional square grid of physical qubits. The two patches are positioned such that their logical X operators face each other and are connected by seven ancilla qubits (gray circles), enabling...

FIG. 2. Characterization of the logical Bell state generated via lattice split. a, Simplified circuit for preparing a logical Bell state. The system is initialized by preparing all data qubits in the logical state |0L3⟩of a 7 × 3 merged surface code. The lattice is subsequently split into two entangled 3 × 3 code patches by measuring the ancilla data qubits. Pauli-frame updates, conditioned on the intermediate measurement outcomes (m1, m2, and m3; see below), are applied during post-processing (dashed boxes). b, Physical operations corresponding to each step in the circuit. Each SEC consists of four layers of CZ gates followed by a round of measurements on the syndrome qubits. To perform...

FIG. 3. Logical implementation of the Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm. a, Simplified circuit diagram of the Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm, which is proposed to distinguish between constant and balanced functions in a single trial. In this two-qubit implementation, the quantum ora- cle Uf is efficiently realized via lattice surgery between two surface- code logical qubits. b, Measured accuracy of the logical algorithm for both constant and balanced functions. Dashed and solid wire- frames represent the ideal values and simulation results, respectively. c-d, Effect of soft post-selection based on argument reweighting (c) and complementary gap (d). Increasing the suppression parameter or gap threshold...

FIG. 4. Implementation of logical gate teleportation via lattice surgery. a, Protocol for logical magic state preparation. For each surface- code patch, the unique physical data qubit participating in both XL and ZL is initialized in the target physical magic state (|ψ1⟩or |ψ2⟩). The remaining data qubits are prepared in |0⟩or |+⟩, such that a subsequent round of syndrome extraction projects the code into the desired logical magic state (|ψL1⟩or |ψL2⟩). b, Circuit diagram for an arbitrary rotation around the logical X axis.
